original_abstract: This article examines manifest interparty conflict over policy issues and the strategic role of coalition agreements in resolving these disputes. We present empirical findings on the structure of coalition agreements—specifically regarding policy compromises—and the resulting interparty conflicts that emerge throughout the lifespan of governments in Germany, Belgium, Italy, and the Netherlands. Our analysis explores the extent to which governing parties are constrained by formal, written agreements on disputed topics. The evidence shows that coalition agreements across all four nations contain specific policy mandates, one-third of which are framed with high precision. While these deals cover both consensual and controversial issues, our central finding reveals that when internal conflicts arise, parties almost invariably revert to the original agreement if a relevant provision exists. Consequently, the policy statements embedded in coalition agreements serve as a vital mechanism for facilitating stable decision-making across diverse political systems.
humanized_abstract: This paper investigates the occurrence of manifest interparty conflict on policy issues and the functions of coalition agreements as a means of conflict resolution. Based on our empirical research into the structure of coalition agreements in relation to policy compromises and the conflicts occurring during the terms of office of coalition governments in Germany, Belgium, Italy and the Netherlands, our analysis provides insight into the scope of binding influence of formally and contractually documented policy issues for the participating parties. Our analysis shows that all coalition agreements in our sample of four countries contain policy obligations for the governing parties, approximately one-third of them in highly specified form. Both agreed and contentious issues are covered, and our core finding is that parties can always refer back to the contractual terms of the agreement in case of intra-governmental disagreement whenever this is possible due to the presence of corresponding clauses in the coalition agreement. Thus the policy content of a coalition agreement has a regulative function that is conducive to the stable operation of parties in very different coalition constellations.
